critic_raw:
The methodology is largely well-constructed — chronological splitting, fit-on-train-only preprocessing, single test-set evaluation, ablation design. However, there is one structural flaw significant enough to undermine the core validity claim, and two secondary issues worth naming.

**Issue 1: Agent history features are a target leak in disguise**

The methodology argues that agent assignment happens before routing classification in the operational workflow, so including agent history is legitimate. But the feature being constructed is 'the historical distribution of resolution categories for the assigned agent' — i.e., what categories this agent tends to handle. In production, agents are assigned *because* they handle certain categories; the resolution category and the agent assignment are jointly determined by the same upstream triage decision. The resolution category label is therefore partially encoded in the agent identity feature. Even if in deployment the agent is 'already attached at triage,' the assignment process itself uses resolution category as a signal (explicitly or implicitly through routing rules). The model is not learning to predict from independent covariates — it is partially reading a noisy encoding of the label from the agent history vector.

The specific failure mechanism: if agent specialization is stable (agent A handles 90% billing disputes), then the agent identity feature alone will recover much of the label. The proposed model will appear to outperform the baseline on macro-F1, but the gain will be attributable to memorizing agent-to-category mappings rather than understanding ticket content. This defeats the stated hypothesis that the model achieves 'deeper issue understanding.'

The proposed ablation (removing metadata including agent history) is the right test, but the design does not commit to reporting that ablation result as primary evidence — it is positioned as a robustness check. If the full model beats the baseline but the no-agent-history ablation does not, the hypothesis claim fails. The design should state this explicitly and pre-register it as a falsification criterion.

Evidence either way: Run the ablation as described. If macro-F1 on the text+metadata-only model (no agent history) matches the full model, the leak concern is moot. If the delta between full model and ablation model is larger than the delta between ablation model and baseline, then agent history is doing the heavy lifting and the core claim is not supported.

**Issue 2: TF-IDF fit on 24 months may not represent the vocabulary of months 31–36**

Product names, error codes, and technical jargon evolve. A vocabulary fit on months 1–24 will OOV-drop tokens that appear only in the test period. This is not a leakage problem — it is a deliberate design choice — but it systematically disadvantages the text features at test time relative to how they would perform if the vocabulary were continuously updated. The baseline (text-only logistic regression) is more affected than the proposed model (which has metadata and agent history as fallbacks). This asymmetry could *understate* baseline performance relative to a production deployment where vocabulary would be periodically refreshed.

Evidence either way: Measure OOV rate in the test set vs. validation set. If it is similar (vocabulary stabilized), this is a non-issue. If OOV rate rises sharply in months 31–36 — especially for specific product lines or issue categories — the baseline is being penalized unfairly and the relative gain of the proposed model is inflated.

**Issue 3: Class-weighted loss on a GBT vs. logistic regression is not a controlled comparison**

Both models use class-weighted loss, but gradient boosted trees and logistic regression respond differently to class weights — GBTs can effectively up-sample minority classes through leaf-level splitting decisions, while logistic regression applies weights to the gradient uniformly. This is not a pure feature-set comparison; it is also a model capacity and imbalance-handling comparison conflated into one. If macro-F1 improves, the design cannot isolate whether the gain came from the richer feature set or from the GBT's superior handling of minority classes.

Evidence either way: Train a logistic regression on all three feature groups (text + metadata + agent history) with the same class weighting. If it matches the GBT's macro-F1, model capacity is not a confounder. If the GBT substantially outperforms it even on text-only features, the comparison is conflating architecture with feature engineering.

**Summary**

The agent history leak (Issue 1) is the most serious problem because it directly undermines the stated hypothesis. The ablation test is already present in the design but not positioned as a primary falsification criterion. Issues 2 and 3 are real but secondary — they affect interpretation of relative gains, not validity of the overall experimental structure.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis

### Critique 1: Agent history features are a target leak in disguise

**Critic's claim:** The historical distribution of resolution categories for the assigned agent encodes a noisy version of the label, because agent assignment and resolution category are jointly determined by the same upstream triage decision. The model is partially reading the label from the agent identity feature rather than learning from ticket content, defeating the 'deeper issue understanding' hypothesis.

**Assessment:** This is a genuine and serious concern. I want to be honest here: the design rationale — 'agents are already attached at triage, so agent identity is a legitimate covariate in the operational workflow' — conflates two distinct things: (a) whether the feature is available at prediction time, and (b) whether the feature is informationally independent of the label. The critic is correct that agent specialization is causally downstream of routing decisions, which means agent-to-category mapping is not an independent predictor but a downstream reflection of the labeling process. In a highly specialized environment (agent A handles 90% billing disputes), the agent history vector is essentially a smoothed encoding of the resolution label.

**Counter-argument and concession:** I partially concede this. The feature is not illegal in a deployment sense — it may genuinely be available at prediction time — but its information content is problematic for the stated hypothesis. The design claims to test 'deeper issue understanding,' meaning the model should be learning from ticket content. If agent history carries the bulk of the predictive signal, that hypothesis is not tested at all.

However, I want to note a partial defense: the severity of the leak depends empirically on how stable agent specialization is and how strongly it correlates with resolution category. In a large support organization with many generalist agents, the agent history vector may be noisy enough that it contributes only marginally. The ablation test already in the design is precisely the right instrument to distinguish these cases.

What I fully concede is the critic's secondary point: the ablation is positioned as a robustness check rather than a primary falsification criterion. This is a design framing error. The correct pre-registered structure should be: 'If the text+metadata model (no agent history) does not match the full model on macro-F1, and the full model's gain over the baseline is driven primarily by agent history, then the hypothesis of deeper issue understanding is falsified.' This should be stated explicitly as a go/no-go condition, not as an optional robustness check. I accept this criticism fully.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether the agent specialization is strong enough to constitute a dominant signal in practice. This is empirically determinable via the ablation.

---

### Critique 2: TF-IDF vocabulary fit on months 1–24 will OOV-drop test-period terminology, asymmetrically penalizing the text-only baseline

**Critic's claim:** Technical jargon, product names, and error codes evolve. A static vocabulary from months 1–24 will miss tokens that emerge in months 31–36. The baseline (text-only) is more exposed to this than the proposed model (which has metadata and agent history as fallbacks), potentially inflating the relative gain of the proposed model.

**Assessment:** This is a valid methodological concern, but I think its severity is overstated as a validity threat. The design intentionally uses a fixed vocabulary to avoid temporal leakage — this is a deliberate and principled choice, not an oversight. The alternative (continuously updated vocabulary) would itself require careful handling to avoid leakage. The critic correctly identifies this as a 'deliberate design choice' rather than a leakage problem.

**Counter-argument:** The asymmetric exposure is real but bounded. In practice, the majority of vocabulary in a support ticket domain is stable over a 12-month horizon — product names and error codes evolve, but the bulk of customer language (problem descriptions, emotional framing, procedural language) is relatively stable. The OOV rate measurement proposed by the critic is exactly the right diagnostic and is straightforward to implement.

Moreover, the baseline is not only penalized by OOV — the proposed model's text component uses the same TF-IDF vectors, so both models share identical text representation at test time. The asymmetry only matters if the proposed model compensates for degraded text features with metadata/agent history, which would further support the ablation analysis under Issue 1. If anything, this concern reinforces why the ablation is necessary.

I concede that the design should include the OOV rate diagnostic as a reported secondary analysis. I do not concede that this invalidates the experimental structure, because both models operate under identical text representation constraints.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether OOV rate rises sharply in months 31–36 for specific product lines or issue categories that are disproportionately represented in certain model comparisons. This is empirically measurable.

---

### Critique 3: GBT vs. logistic regression conflates model capacity with feature contribution

**Critic's claim:** GBTs and logistic regression respond differently to class weights and have different capacity profiles. A macro-F1 improvement could reflect superior minority-class handling by GBT rather than the richer feature set.

**Assessment:** This is a valid concern and represents a genuine gap in the design's ability to isolate the source of improvement. The stated hypothesis is about feature contribution ('deeper issue understanding'), but the design confounds this with architecture choice. A GBT trained on text-only features might already outperform logistic regression trained on text-only features, meaning the feature comparison is not clean.

**Counter-argument:** I partially defend the design on pragmatic grounds: the comparison is not purely academic — the operational question is 'does the full proposed system (GBT + all features) outperform the text-only baseline system (LR + text)?' In deployment, you would use whichever architecture is appropriate for the feature set. However, the critic is correct that this conflation makes it impossible to attribute gains to features vs. architecture, which matters for the stated hypothesis.

The proposed fix is straightforward and I accept it: include a logistic regression trained on all three feature groups as an additional condition. If LR-full matches GBT-full, architecture is not the confound. If GBT substantially outperforms LR on text-only features, that gap should be separately characterized. This is a legitimate gap in the current design.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether GBT's minority-class handling advantage over LR is large enough to materially affect macro-F1 in this specific domain. Empirically testable.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing my Pass 1 analysis:

- **Issue 1 (agent history leak):** Partially conceded. The concern is real and the framing of the ablation as a 'robustness check' rather than a primary falsification criterion is a genuine design error. The severity depends on empirical agent specialization patterns. This is a significant but potentially fixable structural problem.

- **Issue 2 (OOV vocabulary drift):** Partially conceded as a diagnostic gap. Not a validity-destroying flaw given both models share identical text representation. Requires OOV measurement to scope.

- **Issue 3 (architecture conflation):** Conceded. The design cannot isolate feature vs. architecture contributions without an additional LR-full condition. This is a real gap in the isolation of the hypothesis.

The combination of Issue 1 (which goes to the core validity of the agent history feature and the hypothesis test) and Issue 3 (which prevents clean attribution of improvements) means the design as stated cannot fully support its stated hypothesis claim. However, none of these issues are unfixable, and the core experimental structure (chronological split, held-out test, ablation presence) is sound. The question of whether the ablation resolves Issue 1 and whether the LR-full condition resolves Issue 3 requires empirical results.

Given that Issue 1 is a structural flaw that may or may not be fatal depending on empirical agent specialization, and Issue 3 requires an additional experimental condition to resolve, the honest verdict is that argument alone cannot settle these questions.
all_issues_raised: Agent history features encode a noisy version of the resolution label via agent-to-category specialization, constituting a target leak that could explain macro-F1 gains without supporting the 'deeper issue understanding' hypothesis.
TF-IDF vocabulary fit on months 1–24 will OOV-drop test-period terminology, asymmetrically penalizing the text-only baseline more than the proposed model and potentially inflating relative gains.

Using GBT for the proposed model and logistic regression for the baseline conflates model capacity and minority-class handling with feature-set contribution, making it impossible to isolate the source of macro-F1 improvement.
all_issues_adjudicated: Agent history features encode agent-to-category specialization that is causally downstream of routing decisions, constituting a potential target leak; the ablation is present but not pre-registered as a primary falsification criterion, which is a genuine design framing error that survives the defender's partial concession.

TF-IDF vocabulary drift asymmetrically penalizes the text-only baseline at test time; the defender adequately rebuts this by noting both models share identical text representations and the effect is bounded, and the proposed OOV diagnostic sufficiently addresses the concern.

Conflating GBT architecture with the proposed feature set prevents clean attribution of macro-F1 gains to features vs. model capacity; the defender concedes this gap and the missing LR-full condition is a real but unresolved experimental hole.
